cards.q: BFS template essentials?
cards.a: deque + visited set; check visited at enqueue (not dequeue) to avoid re-enqueues; track level via separate counter or by queue-length-per-level loop.

cards.q: LRU cache structure?
cards.a: Hash map (key→node) + doubly-linked list. On get/put: detach node, insert at head, evict tail when over capacity. O(1) get/put.

cards.q: Two-pointer pattern works when…
cards.a: (1) Array is sorted (or you can sort it). (2) Each step provably rules out one end. (3) You're looking for pairs, palindromes, or in-place modification. O(n²) → O(n).

cards.q: Sliding window — when to choose fixed vs variable?
cards.a: Fixed: window size is given (e.g., "max sum subarray of size k"). Variable: window grows/shrinks to maintain an invariant (e.g., "longest substring with no repeats").

cards.q: Binary search "on the answer" pattern?
cards.a: When the problem space (e.g., capacity, threshold) is monotonic — small fails, big works — define a predicate can_do(x) and binary search for the boundary. Example: "smallest ship capacity for D days."

cards.q: Union-Find two optimizations?
cards.a: (1) Path compression in find — point every node directly to root. (2) Union by rank — attach smaller tree to bigger. Together: ~O(α(N)) per op — effectively constant.

cards.q: Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford?
cards.a: Dijkstra: non-negative weights only, O((V+E) log V) with heap. Bellman-Ford: handles negative weights, O(VE). Negatives break Dijkstra's "lock-in once extracted" invariant.

cards.q: Monotonic stack — when?
cards.a: "Next greater / smaller element" patterns. Stack maintains decreasing values; new element pops until order is restored. Each element pushed once + popped once = O(n).

cards.q: Singleton XOR trick?
cards.a: Array has every value twice except one. XOR all values: pairs cancel (x ^ x = 0); singleton remains. O(n) time, O(1) memory.

cards.q: Topo sort failure mode (Kahn's algorithm)?
cards.a: If the produced order has fewer than N nodes, there's a cycle. Cycle-detection comes free.

cards.q: Bit-mask DP — when does it apply?
cards.a: When state includes "which subset of N items is visited" and N ≤ ~20. Represent the visited set as an integer; iterate over all 2^N masks. TSP, set-cover variants.

cards.q: Why deque over list for BFS queue?
cards.a: list.pop(0) is O(n) (shifts every element). deque.popleft is O(1). At scale, the diff is asymptotic.

cards.q: Mark visited at enqueue or dequeue?
cards.a: Enqueue. Mark-at-dequeue lets the same node be enqueued by every neighbor before processing — memory blowup in dense graphs.

cards.q: LIS (longest increasing subsequence) optimal complexity?
cards.a: O(N log N) via patience sort: maintain "tails" array, binary search insert position. Pure DP is O(N²).

cards.q: K-th largest in unsorted N-element array?
cards.a: QuickSelect O(N) avg case, OR min-heap of size K — O(N log K). Beats full sort.

cards.q: Top-K from a stream — data structure?
cards.a: Min-heap of size K. Each new item: if larger than min, pop min and push new. O(log K) per item, O(K) memory.

cards.q: Streaming median — data structure?
cards.a: Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper. Rebalance after insert. Median = root(s).

cards.q: Connected components in a graph?
cards.a: DFS or BFS from each unvisited node. Each start = one new component. O(V+E).

cards.q: Cycle detection in directed graph?
cards.a: DFS with "currently on stack" set. Encountering a node on the stack = back-edge = cycle. Or Kahn's topo sort with count check.

cards.q: For "merge K sorted lists" optimally?
cards.a: Min-heap of K head pointers. Pop smallest, push its next. O(N log K) — strictly better than pairwise merge or full sort for small K.

cards.q: Sliding window — invariant requirement?
cards.a: Monotone invariant: adding to window can't un-break the property once broken. Without monotonicity, sliding window may not apply.

cards.q: Two-pointer technique time/space?
cards.a: O(N) time, O(1) extra space on sorted arrays. Each pointer moves forward at most N times. Strictly better than hash-set on space.

cards.q: Binary search on the answer -- when does it apply?
cards.a: Use it when the answer space is monotonic (a predicate flips false-to-true exactly once). Binary search the boundary, evaluating the predicate each step. Classic: 'min capacity to ship in D days,' 'smallest max page in K splits.'

cards.q: Prefix sums — what they buy you?
cards.a: Range-sum queries in O(1) after O(N) precompute. Difference: prefix[r]-prefix[l]. Use also for "subarray sum equals K."

cards.q: Bit manipulation: x & (x-1) does what?
cards.a: Clears the lowest set bit. Repeat to count bits. Used in Brian Kernighan's bit count.

cards.q: Find the two singletons when every other number appears twice?
cards.a: XOR everything to get x = a ^ b. Pick any set bit of x (e.g., x & -x); split the array by that bit and XOR each half separately. Each half isolates one singleton.

cards.q: Dijkstra fails on negative edges because?
cards.a: Locks in a node's distance once extracted from PQ. Negative edges can later offer a shorter path. Use Bellman-Ford instead.

cards.q: Bellman-Ford complexity + benefit?
cards.a: O(VE). Handles negative edges. Detects negative cycles (one extra relaxation pass — if any edge can still relax, cycle exists).

cards.q: Largest Rectangle in Histogram -- key invariant?
cards.a: Use a stack of strictly increasing bar indices. When a shorter bar arrives, pop and compute the rectangle bounded by the popped bar's height; the width spans from the new top+1 to the current index. Each bar pushed and popped once -- O(N).

cards.q: A* vs Dijkstra?
cards.a: A* = Dijkstra + heuristic h(n). With admissible heuristic (never overestimates), A* finds optimal + prunes search dramatically when target known.

cards.q: Shortest path between two users in a 10M-node social graph -- which traversal?
cards.a: Bidirectional BFS from both endpoints, alternating the smaller frontier. Cuts the explored set from b^d to ~2*b^(d/2), which is decisive on high-fan-out social graphs.

cards.q: Multi-source BFS -- how do you seed the queue?
cards.a: Push every source onto the queue at the start with distance 0 and mark them all visited. The first time BFS reaches a node, that's its distance to the nearest source -- single pass, O(V+E).

cards.q: Shortest path in grid with up to K obstacle removals -- what's the state?
cards.a: Augment the BFS state with remaining eliminations: (row, col, k_left). Visited is keyed on the full triple so the same cell can be reached with different budgets. Still BFS because all moves cost 1.

cards.q: Bus Routes (min buses to reach destination) -- what's the right graph?
cards.a: Build a graph of routes, not stops. Two routes are neighbors if they share any stop; BFS on routes counting hops. Stop-level BFS blows up because each stop has many neighbors via its route.

cards.q: Tarjan bridge-finding -- what's the bridge condition?
cards.a: DFS tracks disc[u] (discovery time) and low[u] (lowest disc reachable via subtree + one back-edge). Edge (u,v) is a bridge iff low[v] > disc[u] -- v's subtree has no alternative route back.

cards.q: Trapping Rain Water two-pointer -- why is the lower side safe to advance?
cards.a: The water at index i depends on min(maxLeft, maxRight). If leftMax <= rightMax, the left side is the binding constraint, so water at L is leftMax - height[L] regardless of what's between. Move the lower pointer; the bound only grows.

cards.q: Sliding Window Maximum -- which structure gives O(N)?
cards.a: A monotonic deque storing indices with strictly decreasing values. Pop from back while incoming value is larger; pop from front when index falls out of the window. Front is always the window max.

cards.q: Subarrays with exactly K distinct integers -- the inclusion-exclusion trick?
cards.a: Compute atMost(K) - atMost(K-1). Each is a sliding-window count of subarrays with at most K distinct, which is monotone and trivially windowable. Direct 'exactly K' has no monotone invariant.

cards.q: Minimum Window Substring -- when do you shrink the window?
cards.a: Maintain a need-count map and a 'matched' counter equal to distinct chars fully satisfied. Expand right; while matched == required, record answer and shrink left, decrementing matched when a char drops below its need.

cards.q: First Missing Positive in O(N) time, O(1) space -- core trick?
cards.a: Use the array as a hash: for each value v in [1,N], swap it to index v-1. Then scan: first index where A[i] != i+1 gives the answer. Values out of range are ignored.

cards.q: Meeting Rooms II -- min rooms via sweep line?
cards.a: Create +1 events at start times and -1 events at end times, sort (ends before starts on ties), sweep and track the running max. Equivalently: a min-heap of end times, peek vs each new start. Both O(N log N).

cards.q: Non-overlapping intervals -- why sort by end?
cards.a: Greedy by earliest end leaves the most room for what comes next; an exchange argument shows any optimal solution can be transformed into the earliest-end choice without loss. Sorting by start can keep a long interval that blocks many short ones.

cards.q: LCA in a generic binary tree -- the recursive pattern?
cards.a: Recurse left and right. If both sides return non-null, current node is the LCA. Otherwise propagate up the non-null side. O(N) with no parent pointers.

cards.q: Validate BST -- common bug?
cards.a: Comparing only against direct children fails on grandchildren. Pass down (lo, hi) bounds at each call, or do in-order traversal and verify strict increase. Inclusive vs strict matters: use strict (<, >) for standard BST.

cards.q: Max path sum in a binary tree -- return-vs-track split?
cards.a: At each node, recursively compute the best one-armed extension (max child gain, clipped at 0). Update a global best with node.val + leftGain + rightGain (path bends here). Return only the one-armed value -- a parent can't use a bend.

cards.q: Build binary tree from preorder + inorder -- what makes it O(N)?
cards.a: Preorder gives the root; locate it in inorder to split left/right subtrees. Pre-hash inorder value -> index so the lookup is O(1), and recurse on index ranges. Without the hashmap it degrades to O(N^2).

cards.q: 0/1 knapsack -- why iterate capacity in reverse for 1D rolling?
cards.a: Each item can be used at most once. Iterating capacity descending guarantees dp[c-w] still reflects the previous row (no item yet), so you don't 'reuse' the item. Ascending iteration is the unbounded-knapsack form.

cards.q: Burst Balloons -- what's the DP state and why?
cards.a: dp[l][r] = max coins bursting all balloons strictly between sentinels at l and r. Pick which balloon i is burst LAST in (l, r): cost = nums[l] * nums[i] * nums[r] + dp[l][i] + dp[i][r]. Last-to-burst pins the neighbors so subproblems decouple.

cards.q: When does a problem call for DP?
cards.a: When the answer has overlapping subproblems plus optimal substructure: same subcalls recur, and the optimal solution composes from optimal subsolutions. If subproblems don't repeat, it's plain divide-and-conquer or greedy.

cards.q: Most common DP bug?
cards.a: Off-by-one in base case or transition indexing -- especially confusing 'index i means first i chars' vs 'first i+1 chars.' Pick one convention and write base cases that fit it. Second-most-common: forgetting to memoize a parameter that varies between calls.

cards.q: KMP -- what does the failure function store?
cards.a: f[i] = length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix. On a mismatch at text position, jump pattern back to f[i-1] instead of restarting. Build in O(M), match in O(N) -- total O(M+N).

cards.q: Rabin-Karp -- why is the hash 'rolling'?
cards.a: Treat the substring as a base-B number mod a prime. Sliding one char right: new_hash = (old_hash - leftChar * B^(L-1)) * B + rightChar, all mod p. O(1) per slide. Verify equal hashes by direct compare to dodge collisions.

cards.q: Longest Duplicate Substring -- which two patterns combine?
cards.a: Binary search on the answer length L, plus rolling hash to test 'does any substring of length L repeat?' in O(N) per check. Total O(N log N), assuming you handle hash collisions (double hash or verify).

cards.q: Floyd's tortoise-and-hare -- how do you find the cycle start?
cards.a: After fast meets slow inside the cycle, reset one pointer to the head and advance both one step at a time. They meet at the cycle's entry. Proof: distance from head to entry equals distance from meeting point to entry (mod cycle length).

cards.q: Reverse Nodes in K-Group -- structural trick?
cards.a: Walk K nodes ahead to verify a full group exists, reverse those K in place, then connect the previous group's tail to the new head and recurse/loop on the rest. O(N) time, O(1) space (iterative).

cards.q: LFU cache -- data structures for O(1) get/put?
cards.a: Hash key -> node, hash freq -> doubly-linked list of nodes at that freq, and a min-freq pointer. On access, bump the node to the next freq's list; evict from the min-freq list's tail. Each op O(1).

cards.q: Word Search II -- why a trie beats per-word DFS?
cards.a: Insert all words into a trie. Run one DFS per board cell, walking the trie alongside the path; stop instantly when no child matches. Shared prefixes are explored once -- complexity goes from O(W * cells * 4^L) to roughly O(cells * 4^L).

cards.q: Sum of Subarray Minimums -- monotonic stack counting trick?
cards.a: For each index i, find PLE (previous less, strict on one side) and NLE (next less, non-strict on the other) using a monotonic stack. Contribution = A[i] * (i - PLE) * (NLE - i). Asymmetric strictness avoids double-counting equal mins.

cards.q: Kth smallest in a sorted matrix -- binary search on what?
cards.a: Binary search on the value, not the index. For a candidate v, count cells <= v in O(N) via the staircase walk from bottom-left. Shrink lo/hi by whether the count is < K or >= K. O(N log(maxVal-minVal)).

cards.q: Minimum Refueling Stops -- the 'borrow from the past' heap-greedy?
cards.a: Drive forward without stopping, dropping each reachable station's fuel into a max-heap as you pass it. When you run out before the next station/target, pop the largest remembered tank and add it -- one stop. Repeat until you reach target or the heap is empty.
